agent1_specific:
You have been working as the leasing manager of this mid-sized apartment complex for four months, after struggling through nearly half a year of unemployment. You are married with two young children, and at $46,000 per year, this is the most stable and best-paying position you have ever had. You are determined not to risk your future at this job. Leasing agents come and go quickly in this competitive market, and you know that one careless mistake could end your chances at advancement. You pride yourself on following company policy closely, while also appearing approachable to potential tenants.

The company has strict requirements for new leases. In order to secure an apartment, every prospective tenant must present valid documents proving employment and income, fill out the lease application completely, and submit the initial deposit on the day of signing. Without the deposit, company policy clearly states that the unit cannot be held. This rule prevents situations where multiple applicants compete for a unit and helps ensure the building meets its occupancy goals each month. Most people who fail to provide deposits on time are simply shopping around or unwilling to commit, and the company discourages entertaining such cases.

You do have access to a modest discretionary allowance — capped at $200 — that the company permits strictly for resolving tenant disputes or covering minor administrative costs. It cannot be applied toward or in lieu of a security deposit under any circumstances. Management prefers to see this allowance unused unless absolutely necessary, and your supervisor made clear in your last performance review that bending the rules too often could signal weakness rather than flexibility. With this month's occupancy nearly full, there is little incentive to take risks with applicants who do not meet every requirement, because another fully qualified renter with deposit in hand could claim the unit at any time once they complete their application.

The tenant you are about to meet toured one of the recently renovated one-bedroom units almost two weeks ago. Since then, you have received two other completed applications with deposits paid for similar units in the building, and one additional prospective renter has explicitly expressed interest in this specific unit and may submit a completed application with deposit as early as tomorrow. Company policy requires you to process applications strictly in the order deposits are received for a given unit, meaning that if another applicant submits a completed lease and deposit before this tenant does, that applicant gets the unit.

The tenant is arriving today with all required documentation but has indicated they cannot pay the full deposit until approximately five days from now. You understand this is a small gap, but policy does not permit you to hold the unit without the deposit, and doing so could directly cost the building a completed lease from another qualified applicant. Your challenge is to communicate the policy clearly and firmly while treating the tenant with enough respect and professionalism that they remain willing to apply here in the future — or, ideally, find a way to move forward today. You are aware that if the tenant can pay even a partial deposit today, it may open a conversation worth escalating to your supervisor, but you cannot make that commitment on your own authority.
agent2_specific:
You are eager to secure a one-bedroom apartment in this complex because it is close to your workplace, walkable to a grocery store, and located in a neighborhood you feel comfortable living in. At $1,450 per month, it is slightly above what you originally budgeted, but the conveniences and amenities make it worth the stretch. You have been searching for weeks and have already submitted applications to two other buildings, both of which handled your paperwork poorly — in one case the property manager never called you back, and in another the unit was given away before your file was reviewed. These experiences have left you anxious and mistrustful of the process, and you want clear assurances that this apartment will be yours if you complete the paperwork today.

You have come prepared with your identification, income verification, and references, all neatly organized. However, you do not have the full security deposit available today. You have saved a portion of it and need approximately five more days before you can access the remainder. You have no other strong housing leads at the moment — your two prior applications have stalled, and you have not found other units that match this one on location, price, and comfort. This is your best option, and you know it.

You have not heard anything specific about this company's policies regarding deposit flexibility — you are hoping they will work with you given your complete documentation and demonstrated commitment, but you do not know for certain whether that is possible. What matters most to you is leaving this meeting with some form of certainty about the apartment. If the manager cannot hold the unit outright, you are open to creative solutions — such as paying a portion of the deposit today and the remainder in five days — as long as you get a firm commitment in return. You are frustrated by your past experiences and may become emotional if you feel you are being dismissed or treated unfairly, but you recognize that professionalism will serve you better than confrontation.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the tenant submit the required deposit, or at minimum a partial deposit with a firm documented plan to pay the remainder, on the day of signing?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the leasing manager successfully follow company policy throughout the negotiation, neither waiving the deposit requirement entirely nor making unauthorized commitments?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the leasing manager maintain a respectful and professional tone throughout, leaving the tenant with a positive enough impression to consider this property in the future?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the leasing manager avoid making unauthorized exceptions — such as unilaterally waiving the deposit, holding the unit indefinitely without a deposit, or misrepresenting company policy — that could jeopardize their job security?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the leasing manager either secure a completed lease with deposit from this tenant, or keep the unit available for the next fully qualified applicant without making competing commitments?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Prospective Tenant secure the apartment unit, either through completing the lease today or receiving a firm conditional commitment tied to a concrete deposit payment plan?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Prospective Tenant obtain a specific, documented accommodation on the deposit timeline — such as a partial deposit today with a firm written date for the remainder — rather than a vague or noncommittal response?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Prospective Tenant receive a clear and specific statement — not a vague reassurance — about the conditions under which the unit would or would not be released to another applicant?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Prospective Tenant feel treated respectfully throughout the interaction, without being dismissed, condescended to, or made to feel their situation was unreasonable?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was the monthly rent kept at or below $1,450 with no unexpected added costs introduced during the negotiation?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Apartment Leasing Manager and Prospective Tenant reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: Both parties are meeting to discuss the potential lease of a one-bedroom apartment in a mid-sized complex that has seen high recent demand. The prospective tenant values the property for its location, amenities, and convenience, and has come fully prepared with the required documentation but cannot pay the full security deposit until approximately five days from now. The leasing manager must balance strict company policy — which requires a deposit on the day of signing before a unit can be held — with the goal of treating applicants professionally and not foreclosing future leasing relationships. The key tension is whether a creative solution, such as a partial deposit today with a firm payment plan for the remainder, can be reached within the manager's authority — or whether the tenant must return when fully prepared, risking loss of the unit to another applicant who is already close to submitting a completed application with deposit.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants recognize how easily a negotiation can devolve into a counterproductive exchange when responses are driven primarily by emotion or reaction, often leading to poor results. Learners will develop an appreciation for the importance of accurately understanding the other party's actual constraints, needs, and motivations — rather than assumed ones — as a pathway to collaborative, value-creating solutions. They will also gain insight into the role of influence tactics such as making threats, displaying frustration or vulnerability, or appealing to sympathy, and evaluate the potential impact, limitations, and ethical considerations of using these approaches. A particular focus is placed on how agents can become emotionally triggered — the tenant through past negative experiences, and the manager through job insecurity and risk aversion — and how managing these reactions shapes outcomes. Ultimately, the case is designed to strengthen participants' ability to identify integrative solutions within real constraints, such as partial deposits or supervisor escalation, and to shift from adversarial stances to constructive problem-solving strategies that produce more sustainable and mutually beneficial agreements.
categories: Anchoring, BATNA, Criteria, Emotions, Identity and Core Values, Intra-Organizational Conflict, Distributive Bargaining, Power Dynamics, Principal-Agent Dynamics, Relationships, ZOPA
title: Deposit Dilemma: Securing an Apartment Without Upfront Payment
